proof:
We prove that Mulan can guarantee victory if and only if \(\theta\) divides \(180\) (with \(0<\theta<180\)).  

---

### Part 1: If \(\theta\) divides \(180\), Mulan can guarantee victory.

**Lemma 1.**  
If a triangle contains an angle equal to \(k\theta\) with \(k\ge 1\), then Mulan can force a win in finitely many steps.  
*Proof.*  
If \(k=1\) she wins immediately. Otherwise she splits the angle \(k\theta\) into \(\theta\) and \((k-1)\theta\) (possible because the angle is at least \(2\theta\)). The two resulting triangles are \(( \theta, \text{other angles})\) and \(((k-1)\theta, \text{other angles})\). The opponent must choose the triangle containing \((k-1)\theta\). Repeating this argument, the multiplier strictly decreases until it becomes \(1\), at which point Mulan wins. ∎

Now suppose the current triangle \(T\) has angles \((A,B,C)\) and that none of them is a multiple of \(\theta\). We show that Mulan can make a move such that **both** possible resulting triangles contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\). After such a move the opponent’s choice will be a triangle with a multiple of \(\theta\); by Lemma 1 Mulan will eventually win.

Write each angle in the form  
\[
A = a + m\theta,\quad B = b + n\theta,\quad C = c + p\theta,
\]  
where \(0\le a,b,c<\theta\) and \(m,n,p\) are non‑negative integers. Because \(A+B+C=180\) and \(180\) is a multiple of \(\theta\), we have  
\[
a+b+c \equiv 0 \pmod\theta .
\]  
Since no angle is a multiple of \(\theta\), we have \(a,b,c>0\). Hence \(a+b+c\) is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\) (it cannot be \(0\) because each residue is positive, and it cannot be \(3\theta\) because \(a+b+c<3\theta\) and if \(a+b+c=3\theta\) then \(A+B+C\ge 3\theta\), contradicting \(180<3\theta\) for \(\theta>60\)? In fact \(3\theta\) would be at least \(180\) only when \(\theta=60\), but then \(a+b+c<180\) and cannot equal \(180\); the only possibilities are \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\).)

We consider two cases.

#### Case 1: \(a+b+c = 2\theta\).
Choose any vertex, say \(A\). Set  
\[
\alpha = \theta - b .
\]  
Because \(a+b+c=2\theta\) we have \(a+c = 2\theta - b > \theta\) (since \(b<\theta\)), so \(\alpha = a+c-\theta > 0\). Moreover \(\alpha = a+c-\theta < a\) (as \(c<\theta\)), and \(A = a+m\theta \ge a\), thus \(\alpha < A\). Put \(\beta = A-\alpha\); then \(\beta = (m+1)\theta - c\). Since \(c>0\), \(\beta>0\) and \(\beta < (m+1)\theta\). Now  
\[
\alpha + B = (\theta-b) + b = \theta,
\]  
so \(\alpha+B\) is a multiple of \(\theta\). Because \(180\) is a multiple of \(\theta\), the angle \(180-\alpha-B\) in the first triangle is also a multiple of \(\theta\). Similarly,  
\[
\beta + C = ((m+1)\theta - c) + c = (m+1)\theta,
\]  
so \(180-\beta-C\) is a multiple of \(\theta\). Hence both resulting triangles have an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).

#### Case 2: \(a+b+c = \theta\).
Since \(A+B+C=180\) and \(a+b+c=\theta\), at least one of the angles must be at least \(\theta\); otherwise their sum would be \(<\theta\). Choose a vertex, say \(A\), with \(A\ge\theta\) (so \(m\ge1\)). Set  
\[
\alpha = \theta - b .
\]  
From \(a+b+c=\theta\) we have \(a+b = \theta-c < \theta\), hence \(a < \theta-b = \alpha\). Because \(A = a+m\theta \ge a+\theta > \alpha\), we have \(0<\alpha<A\). Put \(\beta = A-\alpha\); then  
\[
\beta = a+m\theta - (\theta-b) = a+b+(m-1)\theta = m\theta - c .
\]  
Since \(m\ge1\) and \(c<\theta\), \(\beta>0\) and \(\beta < a+m\theta = A\). Now  
\[
\alpha + B = \theta,\qquad \beta + C = m\theta,
\]  
so as before both triangles contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\) (namely \(180-\alpha-B\) and \(180-\beta-C\)).

Thus, from any triangle with no angle a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan can always split an appropriate angle to obtain two triangles each having an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).  

Now, after this move the opponent chooses one of those two triangles. That triangle contains an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\). By Lemma 1, Mulan can force a win from there. Consequently, if \(\theta\) divides \(180\), Mulan can guarantee victory in finitely many steps.

---

### Part 2: If \(\theta\) does **not** divide \(180\), Shan‑Yu can prevent Mulan from ever winning.

We show that from a triangle in which no angle is a multiple of \(\theta\), every possible move by Mulan produces at least one triangle that also has no angle a multiple of \(\theta\). Since Shan‑Yu can always choose that triangle, he can keep the triangle in a state where no angle is a multiple of \(\theta\) forever. In particular, Mulan can never obtain a triangle with an angle equal to \(\theta\).

Suppose the current triangle has angles \((A,B,C)\) and none of them is a multiple of \(\theta\). Mulan chooses a point \(P\) on a side and cuts to the opposite vertex, splitting one angle, say \(A\), into two positive parts \(x\) and \(y\) (\(x+y=A\)). The two resulting triangles have angles  
\[
(x,\;B,\;180-x-B)\quad\text{and}\quad(y,\;C,\;180-y-C).
\]  
Assume, for contradiction, that **both** of these triangles contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\). Because \(B\) and \(C\) are not multiples of \(\theta\), the only possibilities are:

1. \(x\equiv0\pmod\theta\) and \(y\equiv0\pmod\theta\). Then \(A=x+y\) is a multiple of \(\theta\), contradiction.
2. \(x\equiv0\) and \(180-y-C\equiv0\). Then \(x=p\theta\) and \(180-y-C=q\theta\). Substituting \(y=A-x\) gives \(180-A-C+x=q\theta\), i.e. \(B+p\theta=q\theta\), so \(B=(q-p)\theta\), a multiple of \(\theta\), contradiction.
3. \(180-x-B\equiv0\) and \(y\equiv0\). Similarly leads to \(C\) being a multiple of \(\theta\), contradiction.
4. \(180-x-B\equiv0\) and \(180-y-C\equiv0\). Adding yields \(360-(x+y)-(B+C)=(p+q)\theta\), i.e. \(360-180=(p+q)\theta\), so \(180=(p+q)\theta\). This would mean \(\theta\) divides \(180\), contradicting the hypothesis.

Since all possibilities lead to a contradiction, no move can produce two triangles both containing an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\). Hence every move leaves at least one triangle with no angle a multiple of \(\theta\). Shan‑Yu can always choose that triangle. Thus the triangle will never acquire an angle equal to \(\theta\) (or any multiple of \(\theta\)), and Mulan cannot guarantee victory.

---

### Conclusion
Mulan can guarantee victory exactly when \(\theta\) divides \(180\) (with \(0<\theta<180\)). The set of such \(\theta\) is  
\[
\boxed{\{1,2,3,4,5,6,9,10,12,15,18,20,30,36,45,60,90\}}.
\]
self_evaluation: The proof is rigorous. Part 1 constructs a winning strategy by showing that when θ divides 180, Mulan can always split an angle so that both resulting triangles have an angle that is a multiple of θ, and then by Lemma 1 she can force the multiple to become θ. The case analysis based on residues modulo θ is complete and correctly handles all possibilities. Part 2 shows that if θ does not divide 180, the opponent can always avoid any triangle that contains a multiple of θ, so Mulan cannot win. All steps are justified; no gaps remain.
